title: Bertrand RussellвҖҷs Advice For How (Not) to Grow Old: вҖңMake Your Interests Gradually Wider and More ImpersonalвҖқ
description:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationВӯal PorВӯtrait Gallery, via WikiВӯmeВӯdia ComВӯmons
Advice on how to grow
body:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationВӯal PorВӯtrait Gallery, via WikiВӯmeВӯdia ComВӯmons
Advice on how to grow old freВӯquentВӯly comes from such banal or bloodВӯless sources that we can be forВӯgivВӯen for ignorВӯing it. PubВӯlic health offiВӯcials who disВӯpense wisВӯdom may have good intenВӯtions; pharВӯmaВӯceuВӯtiВӯcal comВӯpaВӯnies who do the same may not. In either case, the mesВӯsages arrive in a form that can bring on the despair they seek to avert. ElderВӯly peoВӯple in well-lit phoВӯtographs stroll down garВӯden paths, ballВӯroom dance, do yoga. BulВӯletВӯed lists puncВӯtuВӯatВӯed by dry citaВӯtions issue genВӯtly-wordВӯed guideВӯlines for senВӯsiВӯble livВӯing. InofВӯfenВӯsive blandВӯness as a preВӯscripВӯtion for livВӯing well.
At the othВӯer extreme are proВӯfiles of excepВӯtionВӯal casesвҖ”relatively spry indiВӯvidВӯuВӯals who have passed the cenВӯtuВӯry mark. Rarely do their stoВӯries conВӯform to the modВӯel of abstemiousВӯness enjoined upon us by proВӯfesВӯsionВӯals. But we know that growВӯing old with digВӯniВӯty entails so much more than diet and exerВӯcise or makВӯing it to a hunВӯdred-and-two. It entails facВӯing death as squareВӯly as we face life. We need writВӯers with depth, senВӯsiВӯtivВӯiВӯty, and eloВӯquence to delivВӯer this mesВӯsage. Bertrand RusВӯsell does just that in his essay вҖңHow to Grow Old,вҖқ writВӯten when the philosoВӯpher was 81 (sixВӯteen years before he evenВӯtuВӯalВӯly passed away, at age 97).
RusВӯsell does not flatВӯter his readВӯersвҖҷ ratioВӯnalВӯist conВӯceits by citВӯing the latВӯest sciВӯence. вҖңAs regards health,вҖқ he writes, вҖңI have nothВӯing useВӯful to sayвҖҰ. I eat and drink whatВӯevВӯer I like, and sleep when I canВӯnot keep awake.вҖқ (We are inclined, perВӯhaps, to trust him on these grounds alone.) He opens with a driВӯly humorВӯous paraВӯgraph in which he recВӯomВӯmends, вҖңchoose your ancesВӯtors well,вҖқ then he issues advice on the order of not dwelling on the past or becomВӯing a burВӯden to your chilВӯdren.
But the true kerВӯnel of his short essay, вҖңthe propВӯer recipe for remainВӯing young,вҖқ he says, came to him from the examВӯple of a materВӯnal grandВӯmothВӯer, who was so absorbed in her life, вҖңI do not believe she ever had time to notice she was growВӯing old.вҖқВ вҖңIf you have wide and keen interВӯests and activВӯiВӯties in which you can still be effecВӯtive,вҖқ RusВӯsell writes. вҖңyou will have no reaВӯson to think about the mereВӯly staВӯtisВӯtiВӯcal fact of the numВӯber of years you have already lived, still less of the probВӯaВӯble shortВӯness of your future.вҖқ
Such interВӯests, he argues, should be вҖңimperВӯsonВӯal,вҖқ and it is this qualВӯiВӯty that loosens our grip. As Maria PopoВӯva puts it, вҖңRusВӯsell places at the heart of a fulВӯfillВӯing life the disВӯsoВӯluВӯtion of the perВӯsonВӯal ego into someВӯthing largВӯer.вҖқ The idea is familВӯiar; in RussellвҖҷs hands it becomes a medВӯiВӯtaВӯtion on morВӯtalВӯiВӯty as ever-timeВӯly as the so-often-quotВӯed pasВӯsages from DonneвҖҷs вҖңMedВӯiВӯtaВӯtion XVII.вҖқВ PhilosoВӯpher and writer John G. MesserВӯly calls RussellвҖҷs conВӯcludВӯing pasВӯsage вҖңone of the most beauВӯtiВӯful reflecВӯtions on death I have found in all of world litВӯerВӯaВӯture.вҖқ
The best way to overВӯcome it [the fear of death]вҖ”so at least it seems to meвҖ”is to make your interВӯests gradВӯuВӯalВӯly wider and more imperВӯsonВӯal, until bit by bit the walls of the ego recede, and your life becomes increasВӯingВӯly merged in the uniВӯverВӯsal life. An indiВӯvidВӯual human exisВӯtence should be like a rivВӯer: small at first, narВӯrowВӯly conВӯtained withВӯin its banks, and rushВӯing pasВӯsionВӯateВӯly past rocks and over waterВӯfalls. GradВӯuВӯalВӯly the rivВӯer grows wider, the banks recede, the waters flow more quiВӯetВӯly, and in the end, withВӯout any visВӯiВӯble break, they become merged in the sea, and painВӯlessВӯly lose their indiВӯvidВӯual being. The man who, in old age, can see his life in this way, will not sufВӯfer from the fear of death, since the things he cares for will conВӯtinВӯue. And if, with the decay of vitalВӯiВӯty, weariВӯness increasВӯes, the thought of rest will not be unwelВӯcome. I should wish to die while still at work, knowВӯing that othВӯers will carВӯry on what I can no longer do and conВӯtent in the thought that what was posВӯsiВӯble has been done.
Read RussellвҖҷs вҖңHow to Grow OldвҖқ in full here. And see many more eloВӯquent medВӯiВӯtaВӯtions on aging and deathвҖ”from HenВӯry Miller, AndrГ© Gide, UrsuВӯla K. Le Guin, and Grace PaleyвҖ”at The MarВӯginВӯaВӯlian.
Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatВӯed ConВӯtent: В 
Bertrand RussellвҖҷs Advice to PeoВӯple LivВӯing 1,000 Years in the Future: вҖңLove is Wise, Hatred is FoolВӯishвҖқ
Bertrand RusВӯsell: The EveryВӯday BenВӯeВӯfit of PhiВӯlosВӯoВӯphy Is That It Helps You Live with UncerВӯtainВӯty
Simone de BeauvoirвҖҷs PhiВӯlosВӯoВӯphy on FindВӯing MeanВӯing in Old Age
YouвҖҷre Only As Old As You Feel: HarВӯvard PsyВӯcholВӯoВӯgist Ellen Langer Shows How MenВӯtal AttiВӯtude Can PotenВӯtialВӯly Reverse the Effects of Aging
Josh Jones is a writer and musiВӯcian based in Durham, NC.В

title: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World: From the Walls of Babylon to the Sewers of Rome
description:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevВӯen wonВӯders of the ancient worl
body:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevВӯen wonВӯders of the ancient world. But you almost cerВӯtainВӯly know that there were sevВӯen of them. In a way, that aligns well enough with the worldВӯview of the Greeks who first made refВӯerВӯence to such a list, givВӯen their near-revВӯerВӯence for that numВӯber. SevВӯen were the strings of the lyre (unless there hapВӯpened to be eight or nine), sevВӯen were the gates of Thebes, and sevВӯen were the вҖңwanВӯderВӯing starsвҖқ in the night sky (if you count the sun and moon). The idenВӯtiВӯty of the wonВӯders was less imporВӯtant than the length of their list, and indeed, as ancient-hisВӯtoВӯry YouTuВӯber GarВӯrett Ryan explains in his Told in Stone video above, addiВӯtions and changes were proВӯposed since the beginВӯning.
The clasВӯsic sevВӯen-wonВӯders rosВӯter includes the HangВӯing GarВӯdens of BabyВӯlon, the StatВӯue of Zeus at Olympia, the TemВӯple of Artemis at EphВӯesus, the MauВӯsoleum at HaliВӯcarВӯnasВӯsus, the ColosВӯsus of Rhodes, the LightВӯhouse of AlexanВӯdria, and the Great PyraВӯmid of Giza, that last being the only one still in exisВӯtence today.
RyanвҖҷs alterВӯnaВӯtive list includes the EgyptВӯian labyrinth at Hawara, which Herodotus conВӯsidВӯered supeВӯriВӯor even to the PyraВӯmids; the TemВӯple of Zeus at CyzВӯiВӯcus, which Pliny the Elder described as lined by gold tubes to let in the sunВӯlight (sureВӯly stripped out as soon as the place fell into disВӯuse); the sewВӯers of Rome, a civВӯiВӯlizaВӯtionВӯal achieveВӯment unto themВӯselves; and the TheВӯater of ScauВӯrus, which, though conВӯstructВӯed out of wood for temВӯpoВӯrary use, seatВӯed an astonВӯishВӯing 80,000 peoВӯple.
Ryan comВӯpletes his sevВӯen othВӯer wonВӯders with the Altar of Horns at Delos, held in myth to have been built by ApolВӯlo himВӯself; the Walls of BabyВӯlon, which actuВӯalВӯly appear on the earВӯliВӯest known verВӯsion of the list; and, finalВӯly, the good old ColosВӯseВӯum. As over-familВӯiar (not to menВӯtion over-toured) as it may be, the FlaВӯvian AmphitheВӯater, as the ColosВӯseВӯum was known in its day, does make for a welВӯcome presВӯence among the ancient wonВӯders, being the only othВӯer one apart from the Great PyraВӯmid that we can still visВӯit today. But if you get into the mood to go marВӯvel at thauВӯmaВӯta, to borВӯrow the Greek word, by no means limВӯit yourВӯself to selecВӯtions already curatВӯed by othВӯers. The world is full of wonВӯders, and your own perВӯsonВӯal sevВӯen may not be far away.
RelatВӯed conВӯtent:
Watch Ancient Ruins Get Restored to their GloВӯriВӯous OrigВӯiВӯnal State with AniВӯmatВӯed GIFs: The TemВӯple of Jupiter, LuxВӯor TemВӯple & More
A WalkВӯing Tour Around the PyraВӯmids of Giza: 2 Hours in Hi Def
Ten Lost Roman WonВӯders: The WorldвҖҷs Longest TunВӯnel, Tallest Dam, Widest-SpanВӯning Bridge & More
How the Ancient Greeks Built Their MagВӯnifВӯiВӯcent TemВӯples: The Art of Ancient EngiВӯneerВӯing
Take a VirВӯtuВӯal Tour of Machu PicВӯchu, One of the New 7 WonВӯders of the World

Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

title: Roman Statues WerenвҖҷt White; They Were Once Painted in Vivid, Bright Colors
description: The idea of the clasВӯsiВӯcal periodвҖ”the time of ancient Greece and RomeвҖ”as an eleВӯgantВӯly uniВӯfied colВӯlecВӯtion of supeВӯriВӯor aesВӯthetВӯic and
body:
The idea of the clasВӯsiВӯcal periodвҖ”the time of ancient Greece and RomeвҖ”as an eleВӯgantВӯly uniВӯfied colВӯlecВӯtion of supeВӯriВӯor aesВӯthetВӯic and philoВӯsophВӯiВӯcal culВӯturВӯal traits has its own hisВӯtoВӯry, one that comes in large part from the era of the NeoВӯclasВӯsiВӯcal. The redisВӯcovВӯery of antiqВӯuiВӯty took some time to reach the pitch it would durВӯing the 18th cenВӯtuВӯry, when refВӯerВӯences to Greek and Latin rhetoric, archiВӯtecВӯture, and sculpВӯture were inescapable. But from the RenaisВӯsance onward, the clasВӯsiВӯcal achieved the staВӯtus of culВӯturВӯal dogВӯma.
One tenet of clasВӯsiВӯcal ideВӯalВӯism is the idea that Roman and Greek statВӯuВӯary embodВӯied an ideВӯal of pure whitenessвҖ”a misВӯconВӯcepВӯtion modВӯern sculpВӯtors perВӯpetВӯuВӯatВӯed for hunВӯdreds of years by makВӯing busts and statВӯues in polВӯished white marВӯble. But the truth is that both Greek statВӯues and their Roman counterpartsвҖ”as youвҖҷll learn in the Vox video aboveвҖ”were origВӯiВӯnalВӯly brightВӯly paintВӯed in riotous colВӯor.
This includes the 1st cenВӯtuВӯry A.D. AugusВӯtus of PriВӯma PorВӯta, the famous figВӯure of the EmperВӯor standВӯing triВӯumphantВӯly with one hand raised. Rather than left as blank white marВӯble, the statВӯue would have had bronzed skin, brown hair, and a fire-engine red toga. вҖңAncient Greece and Rome were realВӯly colВӯorВӯful,вҖқ we learn. So how did everyВӯone come to believe othВӯerВӯwise?
ItвҖҷs partВӯly an honВӯest misВӯtake. After the fall of Rome, ancient sculpВӯtures were buried or left out in the open air for hunВӯdreds of years. By the time the RenaisВӯsance began in the 1300s, their paint had fadВӯed away. As a result, the artists unearthing, and copyВӯing ancient art didnвҖҷt realВӯize how colВӯorВӯful it was supВӯposed to be. 
But white marВӯble couldnвҖҷt have become the norm withВӯout some willВӯful ignoВӯrance. Even though there was a bunch of eviВӯdence that ancient sculpВӯture was paintВӯed, artists, art hisВӯtoВӯriВӯans and the genВӯerВӯal pubВӯlic chose to disВӯreВӯgard it. WestВӯern culВӯture seemed to colВӯlecВӯtiveВӯly accept that white marВӯble was simВӯply pretВӯtiВӯer. 
White statВӯuВӯary symВӯbolВӯized a clasВӯsiВӯcal ideВӯal that вҖңdepends highВӯly on the greatВӯest posВӯsiВӯble deconВӯtexВӯtuВӯalВӯizaВӯtion,вҖқ writes James I. Porter, proВӯfesВӯsor of Rhetoric and ClasВӯsics at the UniВӯverВӯsiВӯty of CalВӯiВӯforВӯnia, BerkeВӯley. вҖңOnly so can the valВӯues it cherВӯishВӯes be isoВӯlatВӯed: simВӯplicВӯiВӯty, tranВӯquilВӯiВӯty, balВӯanced proВӯporВӯtions, restraint, puriВӯty of formвҖҰ all of these are feaВӯtures that underВӯscore the timeВӯless qualВӯiВӯty of the highВӯest posВӯsiВӯble expresВӯsion of art, like a breath held indefВӯiВӯniteВӯly.вҖқ These ideals became insepВӯaВӯraВӯble from the develВӯopВӯment of racial theВӯoВӯry.
LearnВӯing to see the past as it was requires us to put aside hisВӯtorВӯiВӯcalВӯly acquired blindВӯers. This can be exceedВӯingВӯly difВӯfiВӯcult when our ideas about the past come from hunВӯdreds of years of inherВӯitВӯed traВӯdiВӯtion, from every periВӯod of art hisВӯtoВӯry since the time of MichelanВӯgeВӯlo. But we must acknowlВӯedge this traВӯdiВӯtion as fabВӯriВӯcatВӯed. InfluВӯenВӯtial art hisВӯtoВӯriВӯan Johann Joachim WinckВӯelВӯmann, for examВӯple, extolled the valВӯue of clasВӯsiВӯcal sculpВӯture because, in his opinВӯion, вҖңthe whiter the body is, the more beauВӯtiВӯful it is.вҖқ
WinckВӯelВӯmann also, Vox notes, вҖңwent out of his way to ignore obviВӯous eviВӯdence of colВӯored marВӯble, and there was a lot of it.вҖқ He disВӯmissed fresВӯcoes of colВӯored statВӯuВӯary found in PomВӯpeii and judged one paintВӯed sculpВӯture disВӯcovВӯered there as вҖңtoo primВӯiВӯtiveвҖқ to have been made by ancient Romans. вҖңEviВӯdence wasnвҖҷt just ignored, some of it may have been destroyedвҖқ to enforce an ideВӯal of whiteВӯness. While many statВӯues were denudВӯed by the eleВӯments over hunВӯdreds of years, the first archaeВӯolВӯoВӯgists to disВӯcovВӯer the AugusВӯtus of PriВӯma PorВӯta in the 1860s described its colВӯor scheme in detail.
CriВӯtiques of clasВӯsiВӯcal ideВӯalВӯism donвҖҷt origВӯiВӯnate in a politВӯiВӯcalВӯly corВӯrect present. As Porter shows at length in his artiВӯcle вҖңWhat Is вҖҳClasВӯsiВӯcalвҖҷ About ClasВӯsiВӯcal AntiqВӯuiВӯty?,вҖқ they date back at least to 19th cenВӯtuВӯry philosoВӯpher LudВӯwig FeuerВӯbach, who called WinckelmannвҖҷs ideas about Roman statВӯues вҖңan empВӯty figВӯment of the imagВӯiВӯnaВӯtion.вҖқ But these ideas are вҖңfor the most part takВӯen for grantВӯed rather than quesВӯtioned,вҖқ Porter argues, вҖңor else clung to for fear of losВӯing a powВӯerВӯful cachet that, even in the beleaВӯguered present, conВӯtinВӯues to transВӯlate into culВӯturВӯal presВӯtige, authorВӯiВӯty, elitВӯist satВӯisВӯfacВӯtions, and ecoВӯnomВӯic powВӯer.вҖқ
Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:
Why Most Ancient CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtions Had No Word for the ColВӯor Blue
Why Ancient Romans Paid a ForВӯtune for the ColВӯor PurВӯple вҖ” More Than Even SilВӯver
How Ancient Greek StatВӯues RealВӯly Looked: Reseasrch Reveals Their Bold, Bright ColВӯors and PatВӯterns
The Met DigВӯiВӯtalВӯly Restores the ColВӯors of an Ancient EgyptВӯian TemВӯple, Using ProВӯjecВӯtion MapВӯping TechВӯnolВӯoВӯgy
Watch Art on Ancient Greek VasВӯes Come to Life with 21st CenВӯtuВӯry AniВӯmaВӯtion
Josh Jones is a writer and musiВӯcian based in Durham, NC.В

title: 'Lettres DГ©coratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de SiГЁcle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms
description: Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless post
body:
Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless posters and ads, but there was a time when hand-painted promotional signage was needed for retail windows, and business names were often rendered just the same. Of course, itвҖҷs a trade that virtually died out with the advent of new technologies, which made it cheaper and faster to produce public messaging. In the way of LPs and film cameras, though, just because there were new methods in daily use, it certainly doesnвҖҷt mean that the art form doesnвҖҷt live on. 
A new book published by Letterform Archive, Lettres DГ©coratives: A Century of French Sign PaintersвҖҷ Alphabets, celebrates the vivacity and timelessness of French sign painting from the 19th and early 20th centuries. Compiled from lithograph portfolios, which range from 1875 to around 1932, the volume includes more than 150 full-color reproductions of these bold lettering samples. These portfolios once served as catalogue-like albums, providing inspiration for styles and motifs that could be translated onto large billboards and small signage alike.

While decorative painting had been around long before embellished lettering came into vogue, it took a while to catch on. вҖңOnly in the nineteenth century did painters make alphabets into primary decorative elements,вҖқ writes sign painter Morgane CГҙme in the introduction. вҖңIndeed even then, many of the letters painted on signboards, canvas awnings, and vehicles remained simple in form, following inherited models of Roman square capitals and modern variants used in printing type. The new demand for eye-catching signs called for significant change in perspective.вҖқ
Explore more in your own copy, which you can grab from the Colossal Shop.

schema.headline: 'Lettres DГ©coratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de SiГЁcle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms

title: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass
description: Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely l
body:
Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely lethal prehistoric predator. Its feathered body, powerful legs, and huge jaw gave it an advantage as it roamed its territory some 66 million years ago. But it was really its so-called вҖңsickle claw,вҖқ a huge, taloned toe that measures 9.5 inches on the outer curve. 
For artist Grant Garmezy, the ancient creature presented a unique opportunity to render a life-size sculptural version. Specializing in meticulously detailed, accurate representations of nature in glass, he took on the challenge of recreating the DakotaraptorвҖҷs 14-foot length from snout to tail.

вҖңThe fossil record leaves much to interpretation, as details like feathers and posture remain a matter of scientific debate,вҖқ says a statement from GRANADA Gallery, which supported the artist in the project. вҖңThis uncertainty mirrors the artistry of the project itself, where each form is a choice, a vision, and a reimagining of prehistory.вҖқ
Go behind-the-scenes as Garmezy makes the sculpture on Instagram, and explore more of his process on YouTube.

schema.headline: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass

title: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects
description: In the age of the internet, weвҖҷre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain
body:
In the age of the internet, weвҖҷre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain and programs like The Metropolitan Museum of ArtвҖҷs Open Access Initiative. Through a searchable digital catalogue, visitors to the museumвҖҷs website can see hundreds of thousands of objects, many images of which are available for download. And itвҖҷs not aloneвҖ”other institutions like the Art Institute of Chicago, The National Gallery of Art, and The Cleveland Museum of Art, among others, make pieces in their collections accessible to all.
The thing is, digital images donвҖҷt always give us the full picture, so to speak. Even two-dimensional paintings and drawings have unique textures, structural details, and materials that we can only really appreciate in person. This wonвҖҷt ever really changeвҖ”nothing beats the real thing. But one caveat is that even in person, much of the work remains hidden. We canвҖҷt see the backs of oil paintings, for example, and edges are often hidden within frames. Thanks to The MetвҖҷs continued emphasis on imaging, we can now experience every detail in three-dimensional renderings of nearly 140 significant objects in its holdings.
The Temple of Dendur (10th century B.C.E.)
The Met is home to a whopping 1.5 million historic objects, which range from the iconic Temple of Dendur and Impressionist paintings to African tribal ceremonial sculptures and medieval pottery. The museum recently published 3D models of some of these, plus numerous other objects, including nine produced in collaboration with NHK (Japan Broadcasting Corporation).
With careful attention to technical precision and color, these animated renderings are research-grade tools, allowing us to see the objects at any angle. View van GoghвҖҷs brushstrokes closer than youвҖҷre allowed to in a museum, zoom in on a Babylonian cuneiform tablet, and turn an 18th-century Turkish tile over to see its reverse side.
The Met plans to continue adding 3D scans to its online library. Explore more on the museumвҖҷs website.

schema.headline: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects

title: Scale the Dramatic Verticality of Grundtvigs Kirke in David Altrath's Dreamy Photos
description: The construction of Grundtvigs Kirke in Copenhagen took nearly two decades, beginning in fall of 1921 and finally reaching completion in 1940. Des
body:
The construction of Grundtvigs Kirke in Copenhagen took nearly two decades, beginning in fall of 1921 and finally reaching completion in 1940. Designed by Peder Vilhelm Jensen-Klint, it transforms the humble brick into a masterpiece of Expressionist architecture. Its pointed interior arches and vaulted ceiling, stepped crenellations, and hulking exterior nod to medieval Gothic and Romanesque styles while also exhibiting a profoundly modern sensibility.
David Altrath, a Hamburg-based photographer whose work emphasizes urban and architectural elements, captures GrundtvigsвҖҷ details in an atmospheric cumulative portrait. Bathed in mellow, golden light, the churchвҖҷs pale yellow bricks appear to glow, complemented by minimal interior decor beyond some simple wooden furniture and altars.

вҖңThe building feels both austere and atmospheric, especially in the way light moves through the interior spaces and emphasizes the geometry of the structure,вҖқ Altrath tells Colossal. HeвҖҷs always seeking unique relationships between form, light, and atmosphere.
The churchвҖҷs tall vertical lines and symmetry required careful attention to composition in order to capture the buildingвҖҷs architectural geometry, and the interior lighting conditions can vary depending on the weather or time of day. вҖңI enjoyed working with these constraints and trying to capture both the structure of the building and the quiet atmosphere of the space,вҖқ he says.
Altrath is currently photographing modernist and contemporary architecture across Europe. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Scale the Dramatic Verticality of Grundtvigs Kirke in David Altrath's Dreamy Photos
